planting.note: Plant out in warm spring soil after frost.

directions: Grow in acidic, free-draining soil in full sun for the deepest flower color. Pinch growing tips through spring to build density and prune after the main flush. Vivid royal-purple blooms cover the bush through late summer and autumn.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost in a sheltered warm spot.

directions: Plant in rich, moisture-retentive but well-drained soil and keep roots cool with mulch. Feed in spring and trim after flowering to keep growth compact. Dense conical heads of lilac-purple flowers crowd the stem tips in summer and autumn.

planting.note: Plant from a rooted cutting after frost has passed.

directions: Grow this large-flowered selection in acidic, well-drained soil and stake it early since the growth is somewhat lax. Prune hard in late winter to keep it shapely and to trigger a strong flush. Extra-large deep purple flowers appear over a long season from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant out after frost, or grow in a pot moved under cover in cold areas.

directions: This naturally compact selection suits containers and low borders, so plant it in a free-draining acidic mix. Shear lightly after each flush to keep the rounded form dense. Deep purple flowers smother the small mounded bush repeatedly from spring through autumn.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a warm spot or keep potted in cold climates.

directions: A true dwarf, this plant is ideal for pots and edging in rich, well-drained soil. Trim after flowering to hold its neat, low habit. Vivid purple blooms cover the compact mound over a long warm-season display.

planting.note: Plant after frost or overwinter in a container under shelter.

directions: This compact white-flowered selection thrives in pots and small borders with acidic, well-drained soil. Pinch tips early and shear after bloom to keep it tidy. Clean white flowers stud the low bush through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant out after frost or grow in a movable pot in cool zones.

directions: Set this compact selection in humus-rich, free-draining acidic soil and mulch to keep roots cool. Trim after each flush for a dense rounded shape. Flowers open white and mature to soft lilac-purple, giving a two-tone effect through the warm season.

planting.note: Plant after frost into a sunny, sheltered position.

directions: This popular large selection wants deep, well-drained acidic soil and full sun for the best show. Prune firmly after the main flush to keep it bushy and manageable as a tall shrub or small tree. Sheets of rich purple flowers blanket the plant in autumn.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a warm, protected spot.

directions: Grow this pink-flowered form in acidic, free-draining soil with steady moisture. Pinch young growth for density and prune after bloom to shape. Soft rosy-pink flowers cover the bush from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant after frost where the marked foliage gets bright light.

directions: Prized for cream-edged leaves, this selection colors best in full sun with well-drained acidic soil. Trim after flowering and remove any all-green reverting shoots. Purple flowers contrast against the variegated foliage through summer and autumn.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a sheltered sunny position.

directions: Give this selection rich, well-drained acidic soil and consistent moisture through the warm months. Prune after the flush to build a compact framework. Large purple flowers open freely from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant after frost in bright light to bring out the leaf color.

directions: This variegated selection carries cream-and-green foliage that flushes pink in cool weather, and it wants full sun in well-drained acidic soil. Shear after flowering and pull any plain-green reverting stems. Purple blooms stand out against the patterned leaves in the warm season.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings once nights stay reliably above 50 degrees.

directions: Root soft stem cuttings in a moist, gritty mix and pot up once new roots fill the container. Pinch the growing tips a few times early on to build a full, arching shrub, and feed lightly through the warm season. The overlapping bronze-pink bracts that give it the shrimp look appear almost year-round in frost-free spots.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after the last frost when soil has warmed.

directions: Grow from cuttings the same way as the pink form, giving it bright but filtered light for the best bract color. The clear chartreuse-yellow bracts fade in deep shade, so aim for morning sun with afternoon protection. Trim leggy stems back hard in late winter to keep the plant compact and blooming.

planting.note: Move rooted cuttings outdoors once frost danger has passed.

directions: Propagate from stem cuttings and grow in rich, well-drained soil with steady moisture. This selection carries larger golden-red bracts on a slightly taller, more upright plant than the species. Deadhead spent bract spikes and shear lightly to encourage repeat flushes of bloom.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants once the soil is warm and frost is past.

directions: Grow from cuttings in rich, moisture-retentive soil under filtered light. This form produces pure white plume clusters that glow in shaded borders. Keep it evenly watered and pinch young shoots to build a dense frame before flowering.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in full sun to light shade.

directions: Start from semi-hardwood cuttings or fresh seed in warm conditions and grow in ordinary well-drained soil. This large, coarse shrub tolerates hard pruning and can be cut back to keep it in bounds. Dense spikes of white flowers marked with purple veining open through the cooler months.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a sheltered, part-shade location.

directions: Root cuttings in warmth and grow in fertile, moist soil with protection from drying wind. This is a bold, large-leaved shrub that reaches shrub-to-small-tree size in frost-free gardens. Tall golden-yellow flower plumes appear in fall and early winter and are a magnet for hummingbirds.

planting.note: Set out plants after frost in part shade.

directions: Propagate from cuttings and grow in moist, well-drained soil in filtered light. The plant forms white, green-veined bracts arranged in cone-like spikes, with small pink-lipped flowers peeking out. Cut back leggy stems after flowering to maintain a tidy shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and fast-draining soil.

directions: Grow this desert native in sharply drained soil and water sparingly once established. It forms a mound of nearly leafless gray-green stems that carry red-orange tubular flowers over a long season. Prune lightly to shape and avoid overwatering, which causes root rot.

planting.note: Plant out after frost or grow as a container specimen.

directions: Root cuttings in a warm, humid spot and grow in rich, well-drained soil. This compact shrub makes a fine container or greenhouse plant, blooming heavily in cool weather. Small nodding tubular flowers are yellow-tipped and flushed red-orange, resembling tiny fireflies from late fall into spring.

planting.note: Plant after frost as a low groundcover or border shrub.

directions: Grow from cuttings in moist, well-drained soil, spacing plants close to knit into a low mass. Short spikes with greenish bracts open into small orange tubular flowers over a long warm-season display. Shear lightly after each flush to keep the mat dense and neat.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in sun to part shade.

directions: Cuttings root readily in moist soil, making this an easy shrub to establish. It is grown as much for its narrow willow-like leaves, often flushed dark purple on the stems, as for its slender white flower spikes. Prune regularly to keep it bushy and use it as an informal hedge in frost-free areas.

planting.note: Plant after frost in warm, part-shaded ground.

directions: Root cuttings in warmth and grow in fertile, moisture-retentive soil under filtered light. This upright tropical shrub carries loose one-sided spikes of small pink to magenta flowers. Cut back after flowering and feed through the growing season to sustain vigorous shoots.

planting.note: Plant after frost; roots survive light freezes in mild zones.

directions: Cuttings root quickly in water or moist soil, and clumps expand steadily once established. Grow in rich soil with regular moisture; the glossy dark leaves take part shade well. Tall spikes of tubular scarlet flowers open in late summer and fall, drawing hummingbirds and butterflies before frost cuts the tops back.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in a sheltered, part-shade site.

directions: Propagate from cuttings and give this large shrub room, as it can reach small-tree proportions in frost-free gardens. Grow in fertile, moist soil with shelter from strong wind. Branching panicles of lavender-pink to purple tubular flowers appear mainly in the cool season.

planting.note: Plant after frost; mulch roots where light freezes occur.

directions: Root cuttings in moist soil and set clumps in fertile ground with steady moisture and part shade. Upright stems carry glossy leaves topped by loose spikes of narrow red tubular flowers in late summer and autumn. Cut frozen tops to the ground in cold-edge zones and the roots will resprout in spring.

planting.note: Set plants out in spring once hard frosts have passed and the soil has warmed.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed on moist, well-drained mix and press in lightly without covering, since light aids germination. Grow in an acidic, sharply drained soil and water sparingly once established, as this shrub resents wet feet. Expect a haze of small white five-petaled flowers along the arching stems in late spring.

planting.note: Plant out in warm spring weather in a sheltered, sunny coastal or inland site.

directions: Raise seedlings in gritty mix and transplant once they are several inches tall, spacing them well to form a screen or windbreak. This species tolerates salt spray, sand, and drought, making it a workhorse for exposed gardens. Small white flowers cover the twisting gray branches through spring.

planting.note: Set out after frost danger passes in a warm, sheltered position.

directions: Sow the tiny seed thinly on the soil surface and keep evenly moist until seedlings appear. Crushed foliage releases a strong lemon fragrance, and the plant can be sheared as a hedge or grown as a small tree. White flowers open in late spring and early summer among the fine, aromatic leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and free-draining soil.

directions: Start seed indoors in a light, acidic mix and prick out seedlings once sturdy. This adaptable species handles poor and periodically wet soils better than many tea trees. Masses of small cream to pale-yellow flowers smother the arching stems in spring.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Sow surface seed and grow seedlings on in gritty, acidic mix before planting out. The rounded gray-green leaves set off unusually large flowers for the genus. Broad mauve-pink to lilac blooms appear across the shrub in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates damp ground once established.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and keep the tray humid until germination. The lemon-and-citronella-scented foliage is often marketed for its insect-repelling aroma. Small white to pale-pink flowers dot the fine-textured branches in spring.

planting.note: Set out after frost in a warm site with room for the arching habit.

directions: Raise seedlings indoors and plant out where the graceful weeping branches can be appreciated. Prized as much for its silvery cascading foliage and peeling bark as for its flowers. Small white blooms appear along the pendulous stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; tolerant of clay and seasonal wet.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist mix and grow seedlings on until well rooted. The stiff, pointed leaves give this tough shrub a fine prickly texture useful for habitat plantings. White flowers cover the branches through late spring into summer.

planting.note: One of the hardier tea trees; plant in spring and it tolerates wet soils.

directions: Start seed indoors and plant seedlings into moist, acidic ground once established. The silvery, softly hairy new growth gives the plant its common name and cool-climate appeal. White flowers open along the woolly stems in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: A cold-tolerant species; set out in spring in full sun to light shade.

directions: Sow the fine seed thinly and grow on in a gritty mix before planting out. Larger leaves and silvery young shoots distinguish this hardy mountain species. Comparatively large white flowers appear in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, freely draining position.

directions: Surface-sow and keep evenly moist until seedlings emerge. Often grown for its coppery to bronze new foliage as well as its flowers. White blooms open freely along the branches in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; suits damp, sandy or peaty soils.

directions: Sow seed on the surface and grow seedlings on until sturdy. The sharp, needle-like leaves resemble juniper and give a fine feathery outline. White flowers stud the stems in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sandy, sharply drained soil in full sun.

directions: Start seed indoors on a lean, gritty mix and avoid overwatering seedlings. Unusually, the large pink flowers open along older wood rather than the branch tips. Showy pink blooms appear from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; tolerates seasonally moist ground.

directions: Sow the fine seed thinly and keep humid until germination. This species is grown for its comparatively large, richly colored flowers. Deep red to crimson blooms appear in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sandy, sharply drained soil in full sun.

directions: Surface-sow onto lean mix and grow seedlings on with minimal water. This low, spreading shrub bears some of the largest flowers and woody seed capsules in the genus. Big pink, red, or green-tinged blooms open in spring.

planting.note: A hardier species; plant in spring and tolerates damp soils.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist mix and keep lightly shaded until up. This compact alpine-tolerant shrub suits cooler gardens and boggy edges. White flowers cover the fine foliage in late spring and summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates riverbank conditions and periodic flooding.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant hardened seedlings into moist ground. A useful, adaptable shrub for streamside and erosion-control plantings. Small white flowers appear along the arching branches in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and free-draining sandy soil.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and avoid overwatering the seedlings. A variable species often showing attractive peeling bark and papery new growth. White flowers open profusely along the stems in spring.

planting.note: Set container plants out in spring after frost in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant from a nursery-grown cutting, keeping the root ball just at soil level, and mulch to hold moisture. This selection carries deep bronze-tinged foliage and double, ruby-red flowers, so prune lightly after bloom to keep it dense. Rich red double flowers smother the branches in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, sharply drained bed.

directions: Set out a rooted cutting in acidic soil and water sparingly once established. A long-popular selection valued for reliable, weather-resistant double blooms and bronzy foliage. Fully double, dark rose-red flowers cover the shrub through spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a container or the front of a bed in full sun.

directions: Plant this compact cutting-grown selection in gritty, acidic mix and keep it lean and sunny. Its naturally tight, mounding habit suits rock gardens, troughs, and pots with little pruning. Small deep-pink to crimson flowers dot the tiny bronze leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the weeping stems can spill over a wall or slope.

directions: Set a cutting-grown plant on a bank or raised edge so the arching branches can cascade. Little pruning is needed beyond shaping after flowering to keep the weeping form graceful. Single pink flowers line the trailing stems in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in acidic, free-draining soil in full sun.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting and mulch lightly to conserve moisture in the first season. The bronze-flushed foliage sets off fully double pink flowers on an upright shrub. Double pink blooms cover the branches through spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, sharply drained position.

directions: Set out a cutting-grown plant and allow room for its upright, vigorous habit. Grown as much for its rich dark-bronze to purplish foliage as for its flowers. Single pink blooms open against the dark leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in acidic soil with good drainage.

directions: Plant a nursery cutting and water sparingly once the roots take hold. This selection offers dark-tinted foliage and richly colored double flowers on an upright bush. Deep burgundy double blooms appear in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and free-draining acidic soil.

directions: Set out a rooted cutting and mulch to keep the shallow roots cool and moist. Named for its soft, apple-blossom-pink double flowers on an upright shrub. Pale pink double blooms cover the plant in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, sharply drained bed.

directions: Plant a cutting-grown shrub and give it room to develop its full, arching form. A long-cultivated selection popular for reliable, heavy bloom on a vigorous plant. Deep rose-pink flowers smother the branches in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; suits containers and smaller gardens.

directions: Set out a compact cutting-grown plant in gritty, acidic soil in full sun. Part of the low, tidy Wiri series bred for pots and courtyard beds. Bright pink flowers cover the mounded shrub in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low ground cover or informal edging.

directions: Plant this spreading cutting-grown selection where it can knit into a low mat. Its dense, weeping habit and fine foliage suit banks, borders, and large containers. Small white flowers wash over the plant in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the arching branches have room to weep.

directions: Set out a rooted cutting and let the fine, weeping branches develop naturally. Valued for its soft green foliage, graceful form, and heavy spring flowering. Masses of small white flowers cover the arching stems in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to show off the colored foliage.

directions: Plant a cutting-grown shrub and trim lightly to encourage flushes of colored new growth. Grown mainly for its coppery-bronze young foliage that ages to green. Small white flowers appear along the stems in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring near paths and seating so the fragrant foliage is brushed.

directions: Set out a cutting-grown plant near outdoor living areas where the aromatic leaves are appreciated. The citronella-scented foliage is marketed for its reputed insect-repelling aroma. Small white flowers dot the fine branches in spring.

planting.note: Set out young plants in spring once frost has passed into free-draining soil.

directions: Plant into gritty, low-phosphorus soil at the same depth it sat in the pot and water deeply until established. Tip-prune after each flush to keep the plant dense and encourage repeat bloom. Expect the toothbrush-shaped red flowers on and off through much of the year, peaking in cooler months.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: Dig a wide hole in lean, well-drained soil and plant without amending heavily. Water through the first summer, then rely mainly on rainfall once roots have spread. Prickly foliage carries yellow to red spider flowers chiefly in winter and spring, drawing nectar-feeding birds.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low groundcover in a sunny, well-drained bank.

directions: Space plants so their prostrate stems can knit into a soft grey-green mat over well-drained ground. Trim wayward runners to keep the carpet even and avoid phosphorus fertilizers. Pink-and-cream spider flowers cluster along the stems from late autumn through spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm sunny position with good drainage.

directions: Plant into free-draining ground and water regularly until the roots take hold. Light tip-pruning after flushes keeps growth compact and prolongs the display. Curved orange-and-pink flower combs appear nearly year-round in mild climates and are a magnet for honeyeaters.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; tolerates a range of well-drained soils.

directions: Give this vigorous hybrid room to spread and plant it in soil that never stays waterlogged. Prune after the main flush to shape and thicken the canopy. Bright red spider flowers cover the bush through winter and spring and attract nectar birds.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and sharply drained soil.

directions: Set this hardy hybrid in lean, well-drained soil and water until it is established. It responds well to a hard prune after flowering, which keeps it bushy. Waxy red spider flowers appear among fine needle foliage from winter through spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm sunny spot with good drainage.

directions: Plant this tall hybrid where its large flower combs can be admired and give it free-draining soil. Prune after flowering to control height and encourage branching. Long creamy-white toothbrush flowers appear through most of the year and are heavy with nectar.

planting.note: Plant in late spring in a frost-free, sunny, well-drained position.

directions: Choose a warm, frost-free site and plant into soil that drains freely. Prune lightly after the main flush to keep the tall shrub shapely. Large golden-orange flower combs appear over a long season and draw crowds of nectar-feeding birds.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low groundcover in full sun.

directions: Space plants so the trailing stems can spread into a dense groundcover over well-drained soil. It tolerates coastal exposure and needs little more than occasional watering once established. Small pink-red spider flowers dot the foliage for much of the year.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a spreading groundcover in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant on a slope or bank where the vigorous prostrate stems can ramble and cover ground. New growth flushes an attractive bronze before turning green. Deep red toothbrush flowers appear through much of the year among the divided leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Set this compact hybrid in free-draining soil and water until established. Tip-prune regularly to keep it neat and to promote flowering. Large flower combs shade from peach to cream and appear over most of the year, feeding honeyeaters.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this alpine species tolerates cold and frost well.

directions: Plant this cold-hardy species in well-drained soil in sun to light shade. It handles frost and mountain conditions better than most of the genus. Pendulous clusters of rusty-orange to red flowers hang from the branch tips mainly in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with well-drained soil.

directions: Give this upright screening shrub room and plant it in soil that drains freely. Prune after flowering to keep it dense from the base. Red spider flowers appear among prickly holly-like leaves through winter and spring, attracting birds.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low, spreading shrub in full sun.

directions: Plant into gritty, well-drained soil and water through the first season. Its low spreading habit suits banks and border fronts, and light pruning keeps it compact. Deep blood-red spider flowers appear nearly all year and are rich in nectar.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, free-draining position.

directions: Set the shrub in well-drained soil and keep phosphorus fertilizers away from the roots. Prune after flushes to maintain a rounded, leafy form. Vivid red-and-yellow spider flowers appear over a long season and draw nectar-feeding birds.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Plant this hardy shrub in lean, well-drained soil and water until established. It clips well and can be shaped into a dense screen or hedge. Rich red spider flowers appear among fine prickly foliage from autumn through spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low groundcover in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Space plants so the low mounding stems can spread over well-drained ground. It suits rockeries and border edges and asks only occasional water once settled. Small pink spider flowers appear among fine foliage for much of the year.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this hardy species tolerates cold and light frost.

directions: Plant this variable species in sharply drained soil in full sun to light shade. Forms range from low mounds to upright bushes, so allow for its habit when spacing. Tubular flowers in red, yellow, orange or cream forms appear chiefly in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, well-drained position with room to grow.

directions: Give this tall hybrid a spacious, free-draining site and water until it establishes. Prune after flowering to control size and thicken the crown. Large pink-red flower combs appear over a long season and feed nectar birds.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, free-draining bed.

directions: Set this spreading hybrid in well-drained soil and water through its first summer. It tolerates coastal wind and light pruning shapes it neatly. Bright red spider flowers appear among finely divided foliage over most of the year.

planting.note: Plant in late spring in a frost-free, sunny, well-drained position.

directions: Choose a warm frost-free site and plant into free-draining soil. Prune after flowering to keep the tall shrub or small tree bushy. Erect red or cream flower spikes appear over much of the year among soft ferny leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a spreading groundcover in full sun.

directions: Plant on a bank or border front where the low arching stems can cover ground. It is hardy and drought-tolerant once established. Masses of apricot-yellow spider flowers smother the foliage in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a compact shrub in full sun.

directions: Set this dwarf, dense selection in full sun and a well-drained but not bone-dry soil. Its naturally compact habit needs only light trimming to stay tidy. Deep red bottlebrush spikes stud the blue-green foliage mainly in spring and autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun as a compact weeping shrub.

directions: Plant this dwarf weeping selection in a sunny, well-drained spot with occasional deep watering. Trim after flowering to maintain its neat arching shape. Red bottlebrush spikes appear against soft weeping foliage, mainly in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; good as an upright screen.

directions: Plant this upright, slightly weeping form in full sun where a tall screen is wanted. New growth flushes bronze-pink before maturing. Bright red bottlebrush spikes appear in spring and intermittently through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with reasonable drainage.

directions: Plant this vigorous upright shrub in sun and water regularly until it is established. Prune behind spent spikes to keep it bushy and flowering well. Large bright red bottlebrush spikes appear heavily in late spring and again in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun as a screen or feature shrub.

directions: Plant this robust upright shrub in sun and give it room to reach full size. It tolerates damp ground and responds well to hard pruning. Large red bottlebrush spikes appear over a long season from spring into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun; tolerates wet soils and light frost.

directions: Plant this hardy species as a tall shrub or small tree in sun and tolerate of damp ground. Pink-bronze new growth is a feature in spring. Soft cream to pale yellow bottlebrush spikes appear mainly in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this species is notably cold and frost hardy.

directions: Plant this hardy species in full sun to light shade in most well-drained soils. Silvery new growth adds seasonal interest before the flowers open. Soft lemon-yellow to cream bottlebrush spikes appear through late spring and summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with moderate moisture.

directions: Plant this upright species in sun and keep the soil from drying out completely while young. Its stiff narrow leaves and erect habit suit informal screens. Deep red bottlebrush spikes appear mainly in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates both wet soils and dry periods.

directions: Plant this adaptable species in sun where it tolerates seasonal wet and dry alike. Silky new growth precedes the flowers. Pale cream to soft yellow bottlebrush spikes appear through summer along the arching branches.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the best flower color.

directions: Plant this cultivar in full sun and moderately moist soil to bring out its unusual color. Prune after flowering to keep the bush shapely. Mauve-pink bottlebrush spikes that fade paler with age appear mainly in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low spreading shrub in full sun.

directions: Plant this low spreading form in sun where its arching stems can soften a bank or border. Trim after flowering to encourage repeat bloom. White bottlebrush spikes appear over a long season from spring into summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for good pink color.

directions: Plant this cultivar in full sun and reasonably moist soil to bring out the pink tones. Prune behind spent spikes to keep growth dense. Soft pink bottlebrush spikes appear in a strong spring flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Plant this fine-leaved species in sun where its needle-like foliage adds texture. It tolerates a range of soils including damp ground. Unusual green to red bottlebrush spikes appear mainly in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a narrow screening shrub in full sun.

directions: Plant this narrow, upright selection close together for a slim hedge or screen in tight spaces. Its naturally columnar habit needs little pruning. Bright red bottlebrush spikes crowd the branch tips through spring and summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; grown mainly for its foliage color.

directions: Plant this selection in full sun where the vivid new growth shows best against a green backdrop. Light pruning encourages fresh colorful flushes. New foliage emerges bright red and pink before maturing green, with red bottlebrush spikes in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the deepest flower color.

directions: Plant this cultivar in full sun and moderately moist soil to bring out its rich color. Prune after flowering to keep the bush dense. Purple to mauve bottlebrush spikes appear mainly in spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; tolerates damp soils.

directions: Plant this vigorous weeping form in sun where it can grow into a large screen or feature. It tolerates wet ground and coastal exposure. Bright red bottlebrush spikes appear heavily in spring against arching foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun as a large screen or small tree.

directions: Plant this large, free-flowering selection in sun with room to reach tree size. It makes an excellent windbreak or screen and prunes readily. Large bright red bottlebrush spikes appear prolifically in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with reasonable moisture.

directions: Plant this upright shrub in sun where its colorful new growth and flowers show well. Prune after flowering to keep it bushy. Pink-red bottlebrush spikes and bronze-pink new foliage give a long display through spring and summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; tolerates damp and coastal soils.

directions: Plant this striking species in sun where it tolerates seasonal wet soils and coastal conditions. Prune after flowering to keep the tall shrub dense. Large brilliant red bottlebrush spikes with golden tips appear mainly in late spring.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into sharply drained ground.

directions: Plant this compact king protea in coarse, lime-free soil on a slope or raised bed for the drainage it demands. Trim lightly after flowering to keep the low mound dense, and never mulch with manure or high-phosphorus feeds. Large rosy flower heads open on a smaller frame than the species, chiefly in cooler months.

planting.note: Transplant seedlings after the last frost into open, sunny ground.

directions: Start seed in autumn in a sandy medium with cool nights to break dormancy. Give this vigorous upright shrub full sun and lean, acidic, free-draining soil, and prune to shape after bloom. Nectar-rich chalice flowers in shades of cream to deep pink appear mainly from autumn into spring.

planting.note: Move young plants outdoors in spring once frosts end.

directions: Sow in a gritty, well-drained mix and keep barely moist while sprouting over several weeks. Plant in acidic, low-nutrient soil with excellent drainage and full exposure, and shear lightly to encourage bushiness. Furry-tipped goblet blooms in pink to salmon carry the distinctive dark beard from autumn through spring.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out in spring into sharply drained soil.

directions: Grow from a rooted cutting in coarse, acidic, phosphorus-free soil with unobstructed drainage. Water deeply but infrequently once established and prune after flowering to maintain form. Creamy-white flower cups edged with a crisp black-and-white beard open across the cool season and cut well for the vase.

planting.note: Plant seedlings in spring in a hot, open position.

directions: Sow in a sandy seed mix kept cool at night to prompt germination. This fast, tall shrub wants full sun and infertile, acidic, well-drained ground and tolerates light pruning after bloom. Silvery-pink open flower heads with dark stamens appear over a long spring-to-summer season.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into gritty, sunny ground.

directions: Start seed in autumn in a coarse mix with cool nights and good airflow. Grow in acidic, sharply drained soil in full sun and avoid rich feeding. Rounded rose-pink flower heads fringed with white-bearded bracts appear from late spring into summer on a neat rounded bush.

planting.note: Set out young plants in spring in a dry, sunny spot.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in sand and grit and keep cool while germinating. Give it lean, acidic, fast-draining soil and full sun, and shelter from prolonged wet. Large woolly-bearded flower heads in pink to red open over a long winter-to-spring window and prize as long-lasting cut flowers.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into open, well-drained ground.

directions: Germinate seed in a gritty mix with cool night temperatures. This slender, fast-growing shrub thrives in acidic, free-draining soil and full sun. Slim spiralling flower heads in cream, pink, or coppery tones open mainly through autumn and winter.

planting.note: Plant seedlings in spring, allowing ample room to grow.

directions: Sow in a coarse medium and be patient, as this slow tree-like protea takes years to establish. Grow in acidic, sharply drained soil with full sun and minimal water once rooted. Broad silvery leaves back creamy, subtly tinted flower heads that open across autumn and winter.

planting.note: Set out after frost into fast-draining, sandy soil.

directions: Sow seed in autumn in a gritty mix kept cool at night. This upright shrub grows quickly in acidic, low-phosphorus, well-drained soil under full sun. Deep pink goblet-shaped flower heads open through autumn and winter and are excellent for cutting.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into open, sunny ground.

directions: Germinate seed cool in a sandy medium with good ventilation. This low, sprawling shrub needs acidic, sharply drained soil and full sun to flower well. Elongated flower heads with a dark pointed beard, cream to greenish or pink, open mainly through winter and early spring.

planting.note: Plant out in spring into dry, well-drained soil.

directions: Sow seed in a coarse mix and keep nights cool to encourage sprouting. A rugged, wind-tolerant shrub for acidic, free-draining soil and full sun, it is a common parent of garden hybrids. Reddish-brown to pink flower heads with a spicy scent open through autumn and winter.

planting.note: Set out young plants in spring in full sun.

directions: Sow in sandy, gritty soil with cool night temperatures to break dormancy. Grow in acidic, low-nutrient, sharply drained ground under full sun and prune lightly after bloom. Green-to-black flower heads fringed with a striking black beard appear from autumn into spring.

planting.note: Transplant in spring, allowing space for a tall shrub.

directions: Germinate seed in a coarse medium kept cool overnight. This tall, upright shrub grows in acidic, well-drained soil with full sun and low water. Silvery, oblong flower heads with dark bearded bracts open mainly through winter into spring.

planting.note: Plant out in spring into sharply drained soil.

directions: Sow seed cool in a sandy mix and keep it barely moist. Unusually tolerant of limestone, it still prefers well-drained soil and full sun with minimal feeding. Cream to deep carmine flower heads open through autumn and winter on a large upright shrub.

planting.note: Set out after frost into open, sunny ground.

directions: Sow seed in a gritty mix with cool nights to trigger germination. Grow this rounded shrub in acidic, fast-draining soil and full sun with little supplemental water. Cup-shaped flower heads in cream, pink, or crimson, often black-bearded, appear from winter into spring.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Sow seed cool in a sandy medium and expect sprouting within a few weeks. This small, quick-maturing shrub suits acidic, sharply drained soil and full sun. Modest greenish-yellow to pink flower heads open mainly in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Plant out in spring in a hot, sunny position.

directions: Germinate seed in a coarse mix with cool overnight temperatures. This sturdy shrub wants acidic, free-draining soil and full sun with sparing water. Distinctive apple-green flower heads with felted, incurved bracts open through winter and spring.

planting.note: Transplant in spring, giving room for a large shrub.

directions: Sow seed cool in a well-drained mix and keep lightly moist. More tolerant of moisture than most, it still needs acidic, freely draining soil and prefers full sun to light shade. Slim cream-to-pink flower heads open through autumn and early winter on a fast, tall shrub.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out in spring into sharply drained ground.

directions: Plant this durable hybrid from a rooted cutting into coarse, acidic, low-phosphorus soil with excellent drainage. It is one of the toughest, most productive proteas for cut flowers, needing only full sun and occasional deep watering. Soft pink goblet flower heads bloom over a long autumn-to-spring season and last remarkably well cut.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into gritty soil.

directions: Grow from a rooted cutting in acidic, sharply drained soil under full sun. This vigorous hybrid needs little water once established and resents rich fertilizer. Rosy-pink flower heads with pointed, felty bracts open through the cool season and hold well as cut stems.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting in coarse, acidic, well-drained soil and site in full sun. Water deeply but rarely once established, and avoid phosphorus-rich feeds. Large soft-pink flower heads with a plush bearded fringe open mainly through autumn and winter.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into fast-draining soil.

directions: Establish from a rooted cutting in acidic, gritty, sharply drained soil in full sun. Give minimal water once settled and keep away from lawn fertilizers. Deep pink to red goblet flower heads open across the cooler months and make long-lasting cut flowers.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out in spring in a sunny bed.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting into coarse, acidic, well-drained soil in full sun. This robust hybrid tolerates a range of conditions but demands sharp drainage and low feeding. Large silvery-pink flower heads open through autumn and winter and are grown widely for the cut trade.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into gritty soil.

directions: Grow from a rooted cutting in coarse, acidic, sharply drained soil in full sun. Pinch young growth to build a dense frame and water deeply but seldom once established. Bright clear-yellow pincushion flower heads cover the shrub from late winter into spring.

planting.note: Set out young plants in spring, allowing room to spread.

directions: Sow seed cool in a sandy medium with good airflow. This tall, silvery shrub needs acidic, sharply drained soil and full sun and is among the more frost-tolerant pincushions. Curved red-to-orange styles sweep back from each flower head like tiny rockets from spring into summer.

planting.note: Transplant in spring, allowing space for a large shrub.

directions: Germinate seed in a coarse mix kept cool overnight. This large, tree-like pincushion wants acidic, free-draining soil and full sun with minimal water once established. Bright yellow-to-orange pincushion heads top a robust, gray-green shrub in spring and early summer.

planting.note: Set out young plants in spring in full sun.

directions: Sow seed cool in a gritty mix and keep barely moist. A hardy, adaptable pincushion for acidic, well-drained soil and full sun, it resprouts from a woody base after cutting. Yellow flower heads ageing to orange open over a long spring-to-summer season.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into sharply drained ground.

directions: Sow seed with cool nights in a coarse medium. This tough, low, spreading pincushion suits acidic, well-drained soil and full sun. Small clustered flower heads shift from yellow to orange and crimson as they age, mainly through spring.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a hot, sunny position.

directions: Germinate seed cool in a sandy mix with good ventilation. Grow this upright pincushion in acidic, sharply drained soil and full sun with little water once rooted. Large spinning flower heads with curled orange styles resemble a firework and open in spring and early summer.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Sow seed cool in a coarse medium and keep lightly moist. This compact pincushion needs acidic, fast-draining soil and full sun with sparing water. Delicate flower heads blend salmon-pink and coral with slender arching styles, opening in spring.

planting.note: Set out young plants in spring in full sun.

directions: Sow seed with cool overnight temperatures in a gritty mix. This glossy-leaved shrub wants acidic, sharply drained soil and full sun and is a key parent of hybrid pincushions. Large orange-to-red flower heads with a coppery cast open in spring and early summer.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into open, sunny ground.

directions: Germinate seed cool in a sandy medium. This graceful, spreading shrub with narrow leaves needs acidic, well-drained soil and full sun. Yellow-to-orange or red pincushion flower heads with long slender styles open from late winter into spring.

planting.note: Set out young plants in spring in full sun.

directions: Sow seed with cool nights in a coarse mix and keep barely moist. Grow this rounded shrub in acidic, sharply drained soil and full sun with low water once established. Bright orange-red pincushion heads with prominent curved styles open in spring and early summer.

planting.note: Transplant in spring, allowing room for a large shrub.

directions: Sow seed cool in a gritty medium. Tolerant of coastal limestone, it still prefers acidic, well-drained soil and full sun with minimal water. Yellow flower heads deepening to orange open over spring on a vigorous, upright shrub.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into gritty soil.

directions: Grow from a rooted cutting in acidic, sharply drained soil under full sun. Pinch growing tips early to shape a full plant and water deeply but seldom. Bicolored flower heads with crimson-and-yellow styles cover the shrub from late winter into spring.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out in spring in full sun.

directions: Establish from a rooted cutting in coarse, acidic, well-drained soil in full sun. This productive hybrid is popular for cut flowers and asks only sharp drainage and low feeding. Golden-yellow pincushion heads open profusely from late winter into spring.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into fast-draining soil.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting in acidic, gritty, sharply drained soil in full sun. Tip-prune young plants for density and water deeply but infrequently once settled. Rich orange-red pincushion flower heads appear in abundance through late winter and spring.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out in spring in a sunny bed.

directions: Grow from a rooted cutting in acidic, well-drained soil under full sun with sparing water. Keep clear of phosphorus fertilizers, which can damage the roots. Flame-toned flower heads shifting through yellow, orange, and red open over a long spring display.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into gritty soil.

directions: Grow from a rooted cutting in acidic, sharply drained soil in full sun. Pinch early growth to build a rounded plant and water deeply but rarely once established. Deep red pincushion flower heads open freely from late winter into spring and last well cut.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out in spring, allowing room to spread.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting in coarse, acidic, well-drained soil in full sun. This large, vigorous hybrid needs space and sharp drainage and dislikes rich feeding. Big orange-red pincushion flower heads open across late winter and spring on a broad shrub.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in full sun.

directions: Grow from a rooted cutting in acidic, gritty, sharply drained soil under full sun. Tip-prune when young for a fuller habit and water deeply but seldom. Flower heads open in warm sunrise tones, blending yellow through orange, over late winter and spring.

planting.note: Set out established plants in spring once nights stay above freezing.

directions: Plant in acidic, humus-rich soil with the root ball level with the surrounding grade and mulch to keep roots cool and moist. Feed with an acid-forming fertilizer in spring and keep evenly watered, since drought stress causes bud drop. Waxy white flowers open in late spring and perfume the garden most heavily in the evening.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost so roots settle before summer heat.

directions: Dig a wide hole in acidic soil and set the plant slightly high to avoid crown rot, then water deeply and mulch. This selection produces large fully double blooms over an unusually long season from late spring into early autumn. Deadhead spent flowers to keep the plant tidy and encourage repeat bud set.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a groundcover or edging once frost danger passes.

directions: Space these low spreading plants close together in acidic, well-drained soil to form a fragrant evergreen carpet. Keep the shallow roots consistently moist and mulched, and shear lightly after flowering to maintain a dense mat. Small double white flowers appear in early summer against glossy narrow foliage.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a sheltered, partly shaded bed.

directions: Plant in rich acidic soil with excellent drainage and give it room to reach its full upright size. One of the oldest garden gardenias, it bears very large double flowers up to four inches across in late spring and early summer. Prune lightly right after the main bloom flush to shape the plant without cutting off next year's buds.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its long bloom season can be enjoyed up close.

directions: Provide moist acidic soil and morning sun with protection from harsh afternoon heat. This compact old cultivar flowers reliably over a long stretch of the warm season and does well in containers that can be brought indoors in cold regions. Pinch spent blooms and feed lightly through summer to sustain repeat flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; buds resist late frost better than older types.

directions: Set the plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to buffer the roots against temperature swings. Its narrow foliage and cold-tolerant buds let it flower dependably where late frosts damage other gardenias. Double white blooms appear in late spring and often repeat sporadically through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; one of the hardier single-flowered gardenias.

directions: Plant this low mounding shrub in acidic soil with good drainage and mulch the crown for winter. Unlike the double-flowered types it bears single star-shaped white blooms with a golden center and heavy fragrance. Flowers open in early summer, so avoid heavy pruning that would remove developing buds.

planting.note: Plant in spring; bred for cold tolerance and repeat bloom.

directions: Give this spreading dwarf acidic, moisture-retentive soil and a mulched root zone for winter protection. It is among the cold-hardiest gardenias and reblooms from late spring through autumn with fully double white flowers. Trim lightly after the first flush and keep evenly watered to sustain later waves of bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring; prized for very large, early flowers.

directions: Plant in rich acidic soil in a spot with morning sun and afternoon shade in hot climates. Also sold as 'Aimee', it opens some of the largest gardenia flowers early in the season with a strong repeat flush in fall. Keep soil consistently moist and feed with an acid fertilizer to support its heavy bud load.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered bed.

directions: Set the plant high in acidic, humus-rich soil and mulch to conserve moisture around the roots. It produces very large, thick-petaled double flowers on a robust upright frame in late spring and early summer. Water deeply and avoid letting the soil dry out, since fluctuating moisture triggers bud drop.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its big blooms are visible from a path or patio.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil enriched with organic matter and keep the roots cool with mulch. This old favorite bears large camellia-shaped double flowers in early summer on a broad, vigorous shrub. Prune only just after flowering, since buds form on growth made the previous year.

planting.note: Plant in spring to enjoy its color-changing blooms.

directions: Grow in acidic, moisture-retentive soil with protection from scorching afternoon sun. Its double flowers open pure white and gradually age to a warm golden yellow, giving two colors on the plant at once. Keep watering steady through the summer bloom period and mulch to hold soil moisture.

planting.note: Plant in spring in containers or the front of a bed.

directions: Plant this neat compact selection in acidic soil with good drainage and keep it evenly moist. It forms a tidy mound and carries many small, fragrant double flowers over a long summer season. Its modest size suits pots and low hedges, needing only light shaping after bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a container or small-space garden.

directions: Give this miniature gardenia acidic potting mix or bed soil and steady moisture at its shallow roots. It stays very low and compact while producing single white star-shaped flowers with a sweet scent in early summer. Its tiny stature makes it ideal for containers, edging, and rock gardens.

planting.note: Plant in spring; hardier and earlier-blooming than many types.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to protect the roots through cold spells. This upright cultivar flowers early on the season with single to loosely double white blooms and shows good cold tolerance. Water regularly during establishment and prune lightly right after the main flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring; selected for cold hardiness and long bloom.

directions: Set this vigorous shrub in acidic soil with good drainage and mulch the base for winter. It is notably cold tolerant and reblooms from late spring into fall with fragrant double white flowers. Keep young plants evenly watered and shape lightly after the first heavy flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compact reblooming Southern selection.

directions: Plant in rich acidic soil and mulch to keep the shallow roots cool and moist. This rounded shrub reblooms through summer and into fall with fragrant double white flowers against dense glossy foliage. Its tidy habit suits foundation beds and low hedges with only light seasonal pruning.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a long-blooming, upright grower.

directions: Give this upright shrub acidic, well-drained soil and even moisture during the growing season. It flowers over a very long window from late spring into fall with intensely fragrant double white blooms. Feed with an acid fertilizer in spring and deadhead to encourage continued bud set.

planting.note: Plant in spring; compact and reblooming for small gardens.

directions: Plant this dwarf selection in acidic soil with good drainage and mulch to hold moisture. It stays compact and blooms from early summer well into fall, making it a strong choice for containers and low borders. Keep it evenly watered and trim lightly after the first flush to keep the habit dense.

planting.note: Plant in spring as an upright accent or narrow hedge.

directions: Site this naturally narrow, upright gardenia in acidic soil and mulch to keep the roots evenly moist. Its columnar habit makes it useful for tight spaces and screening, and it carries single star-shaped white flowers over a long season. Little pruning is needed beyond removing wayward shoots to preserve the tall, slim form.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its patterned leaves get bright indirect light.

directions: Grow in acidic, well-drained soil with morning sun that highlights the cream-and-green marbled foliage. The variegated leaves provide interest year-round, and fragrant double white flowers open through the summer. Remove any all-green reverting shoots promptly so the variegation stays strong.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low groundcover in mild climates.

directions: Space these prostrate plants to knit into a dense, fragrant evergreen mat in acidic soil. Keep the shallow roots consistently moist and mulched, and shear after bloom to maintain a flat, tidy carpet. Small double white flowers scent the garden in early summer above glossy dark foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring; grown for its simple single flowers.

directions: Plant in acidic soil rich in organic matter and keep the roots cool and evenly moist with mulch. Unlike the ruffled double types it bears single, daisy-like white flowers with a bright golden center and strong perfume. Blooms open in early summer, so hold pruning until just after the flowers fade.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a container or the front of a shaded border.

directions: Grow this low, spreading dwarf in acidic soil with steady moisture and bright indirect light to keep the leaf edges creamy. It combines a compact trailing habit with white-margined foliage and small fragrant double flowers in early summer. Pinch out any solid-green shoots so the variegation does not fade.

planting.note: Plant in spring; noted for cold tolerance and repeat bloom.

directions: Set this hardy selection in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch the roots against winter cold. It offers good cold tolerance along with a long season of fragrant double white flowers beginning in late spring. Water steadily through summer and prune only lightly after the main flush to protect next year's buds.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered site with rich soil.

directions: Plant in acidic, humus-rich soil kept evenly moist, and set the crown slightly above grade. This classic florist-type gardenia produces large, thick, sweetly scented double flowers prized for cutting in late spring and early summer. Feed with an acid fertilizer and mulch to keep the roots cool and productive.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a hardy single-flowered form.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to buffer the roots through cold weather. It bears single white flowers with neatly overlapping petals arranged like a pinwheel and shows good cold hardiness. Keep evenly watered during establishment and prune lightly right after the early-summer bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, frost-free site with room to grow tall.

directions: Give this large South African species deep, well-drained soil and space to reach small-tree proportions. It carries single, long-tubed white star flowers with a rich scent, followed by hard woody fruits that persist for years. It is more drought tolerant than the common gardenia once established, needing only occasional deep watering.

planting.note: Plant only after all cold has passed; grow in pots where frost occurs.

directions: Plant in rich, well-drained tropical soil in full sun to light shade and keep it warm and evenly moist. This is the fragrant tiare flower of the Pacific islands, producing pinwheel-shaped white blooms on and off through the warm season. In cool climates grow it in a container that can move indoors, since it has no frost tolerance.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a frost-free, sunny site; drought tolerant once set.

directions: Grow this African savanna shrub in well-drained soil in full sun, where it tolerates heat and dry spells better than most gardenias. It opens white flowers that age to creamy yellow, followed by distinctive gray-green fruits eaten by wildlife. Water occasionally to establish, then let it rely largely on natural rainfall.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants after the last frost against a warm wall or trellis.

directions: Plant against a sunny wall or sturdy support and tie in the twining stems as they grow. Prune lightly after flowering to keep the framework tidy and encourage fresh shoots. Fragrant white star flowers open through summer into early autumn.

planting.note: Plant out after frost danger passes in a warm, sheltered spot.

directions: Give this vigorous scrambler a trellis or fence and rich, well-drained soil. Feed during the growing season and prune after the main flush to control size. The large, intensely perfumed white blooms are prized for oils and appear from summer into fall.

planting.note: Set out in warm weather; container growing suits cold-winter gardens.

directions: Plant in rich, moisture-retentive but well-drained soil and keep it warm. This single-flowered form blooms almost continuously in heat, so feed regularly to fuel the display. Cut sprigs when the round white buds are just opening for the strongest fragrance.

planting.note: Grow in a warm border or large pot moved under cover for winter.

directions: This slow, compact form bears fully double flowers resembling tiny white roses. Grow in fertile, free-draining soil, keep it evenly moist, and shelter from cold winds. The heavy double blooms are long-lasting and richly scented in the warm season.

planting.note: Plant in warm weather or grow in a container to overwinter indoors.

directions: Recognizable by its elongated, twirled petals, this form wants warmth, sun, and rich soil. Pinch shoots to encourage branching and feed through the growing season. The slender, ribbon-like white flowers carry a strong, sweet scent in summer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or autumn; tolerates a range of positions.

directions: Grow this arching, unscented species as a lax shrub or train it against a wall. It thrives in most soils and can cascade over banks where stems root as they touch soil. Bright yellow flowers stud the bare green stems from late winter before the leaves.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a sheltered spot; wall-training suits cooler areas.

directions: Give this large, arching evergreen room to sprawl or tie it to a support. Prune after bloom to remove old flowered wood and keep it dense. The semi-double, pale yellow flowers open in spring and are lightly scented.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil and full sun.

directions: Grow this bushy, self-supporting shrub in well-drained soil where it needs little fussing once established. Prune lightly to shape after flowering. Clusters of fragrant yellow flowers appear through summer, sometimes followed by black berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, sheltered border.

directions: This selection carries larger, more strongly scented golden flowers than the species. Grow in free-draining soil and prune only to shape after the main flush. Fragrant yellow blooms cover the shrub through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in dry, sunny, well-drained ground.

directions: A tough Mediterranean shrub for hot, dry sites and poor soils. Once rooted it tolerates drought and needs only occasional shaping. Small yellow flowers appear over a long summer season, followed by shiny black berries.

planting.note: Plant in warm weather in a frost-free, sheltered position.

directions: Distinguished by its soft, downy leaves and stems, this shrub sprawls or climbs with support. Grow in warm, humid conditions and rich soil. Masses of white, faintly scented star flowers open mainly in winter and spring.

planting.note: Plant in warm weather in fertile, moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Widely grown across South Asia for its perfumed flowers used in garlands. Give it warmth, sun, and steady feeding for a long bloom season. The small white flowers are intensely fragrant and open mainly through the rainy and warm months.

planting.note: Plant in a warm, sheltered spot or grow in a conservatory pot.

directions: Train this vigorous twiner on a support in a frost-free, sunny position. Keep the soil evenly moist during growth and prune after flowering. Purple-tinted buds open to sweetly scented white flowers over a long summer-to-autumn season.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a support in sun or light shade.

directions: Unusual among jasmines for its deep pinkish-red flowers on a hardy, twining frame. Give it a trellis and moist, well-drained soil, and prune after bloom. The small, fragrant rosy flowers appear in early summer, sometimes followed by black berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a warm wall or arch in fertile soil.

directions: This vigorous hybrid bears soft pink flowers, a cross between the common and red jasmines. Train it on a support and prune after flowering to control its size. Clusters of fragrant pale-pink blooms open through summer.

planting.note: Plant in warm weather in a frost-free, sunny position.

directions: Grow this glossy-leaved semi-climber on a support or let it mound as a loose shrub. It needs warmth and well-drained soil, and responds to light shaping. Purple-backed buds open to fragrant, pinwheel-shaped white flowers over the warm season.

planting.note: Plant in warm weather; can be aggressive, so site with care.

directions: A vigorous, glossy-leaved twiner that flowers freely in warm climates. Provide a strong support and expect to prune regularly to keep it in bounds. Pink buds open to fragrant white flowers nearly year-round in the tropics.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a rock garden, trough, or free-draining raised bed.

directions: This tiny, twiggy jasmine forms a low mound ideal for troughs and rock gardens. Grow in gritty, sharply drained soil and give it full sun. Small yellow flowers dot the dense green cushion in early summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost on a support; site in sun for best leaf color.

directions: Grown mainly for its bright golden-yellow foliage that lights up a wall or fence. Give it sun to keep the leaf color vivid and tie in the twining stems. Fragrant white flowers appear in summer against the gold leaves.

planting.note: Plant after frost against a warm, sheltered support.

directions: This selection carries larger, creamy-white flowers with a rich scent. Grow on a trellis in fertile, well-drained soil and prune lightly after bloom. The broad, buttery flowers open through summer into early autumn.

planting.note: Plant after frost on a support; shelter from harsh midday sun.

directions: Prized for grey-green leaves edged in creamy white that brighten a shady wall. Grow on a support in fertile soil and prune after flowering to keep it neat. Fragrant white flowers appear in summer above the variegated foliage.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or autumn against a wall or bank.

directions: A yellow-mottled leaf form of winter jasmine grown for its variegated foliage. Train the arching stems on a wall or let them cascade, and prune after flowering. Bright yellow flowers open on bare stems in the depths of winter.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a warm, sunny, sheltered spot.

directions: An evergreen shrub with a sprawling habit that tolerates heat and some drought. Grow in well-drained soil and prune lightly to shape after flowering. Fragrant yellow flowers, with a scent said to recall several fruits, open over a long warm season.

planting.note: Plant in warm weather in a frost-free, sunny position.

directions: A scrambling evergreen from southern Africa that climbs with support or mounds as a shrub. Give it warmth and well-drained soil, and thin old stems after bloom. Clusters of fragrant white flowers appear mainly in late summer and autumn.

planting.note: Plant in warm weather in a frost-free, sheltered spot.

directions: A glossy-leaved twining shrub for warm, frost-free gardens. Provide a support and well-drained soil, and prune after flowering to control spread. Fragrant white star flowers open over the warmer months.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a spot with room for a large arching shrub.

directions: Dig a hole twice as wide as the container and backfill with the native soil amended for drainage. This burgundy-leaved variety colors best in full sun and holds its foliage tone year-round in mild climates. Hot-pink fringe flowers cover the branches heavily in spring with scattered rebloom through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its tall upright form has vertical room.

directions: Site this vigorous grower in full sun for the deepest purple-black leaf color and give it acidic soil. Prune right after the main spring flush if you want to keep its naturally tall, upright habit in bounds. Rich fuchsia-pink fringe blooms open in early spring against nearly black foliage.
